q: Without any anatomical specialisations, the gross surface area of the human small intestine would be approximately:
options: 3.3 m²
200 m²
20 m²
explain: The unmodified gross surface area of the small intestine is ~0.33 m² (equivalent to 7–8 book pages). The true absorptive surface reaches ~200 m² only after amplification by circular folds (×3), villi (×10), and microvilli (×20). 3.3 m² and 20 m² are plausible distractors but are not documented values.

q: Which of the following dietary carbohydrates is classified as indigestible and is excreted in feces?
options: Cellulose
Sucrose
Lactose
Maltose
Starch
explain: Cellulose is a dietary fibre; humans lack the enzyme to cleave its β-1,4 glycosidic bonds. Sucrose, lactose, and maltose are digestible disaccharides, and starch is a digestible polysaccharide — all are broken down and absorbed.

q: Oral Rehydration Therapy (ORT) reduces diarrhoea mortality by ~93%. Which mechanism is the primary reason it works?
options: Glucose and Na⁺ are co-transported via SGLT1, and water follows osmotically
Glucose stimulates secretion of anti-diarrhoeal hormones that seal tight junctions
KCl replaces lost potassium and stops intestinal secretion
Sodium citrate alkalinises the lumen, killing pathogenic bacteria
Glucose is metabolised to ATP which powers Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase directly
explain: ORT exploits SGLT1: glucose drives coupled Na⁺ entry into absorptive cells even during active diarrhoea, and water follows by osmosis. KCl and sodium citrate maintain electrolyte balance but are not the primary mechanism for water recovery.

q: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are absorbed by which mechanism?
options: Passive diffusion incorporated into mixed micelles along with dietary lipids
Na⁺-dependent carrier proteins in the brush border
Binding to intrinsic factor, then receptor-mediated endocytosis
Active transport driven by inward H⁺ flux via PEPT1
Aquaporin-mediated facilitated diffusion
explain: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are hydrophobic and are incorporated into mixed micelles together with lipid digestion products, then passively diffuse into absorptive cells. Na⁺-dependent carriers are for free amino acids; intrinsic factor is unique to B12; PEPT1 handles di/tripeptides.

q: At HIGH dietary calcium intake, Ca²⁺ absorption in the small intestine occurs via which mechanism and location?
options: Paracellular passive diffusion through tight junctions throughout the small intestine
Transcellular active transport regulated by calcitriol in the duodenum only
Facilitated diffusion via calbindin across the entire intestinal mucosa
Ca²⁺-ATPase-driven active transport at the brush border of the jejunum
Endocytosis of Ca²⁺-containing vesicles in the ascending colon
explain: When dietary Ca²⁺ is high, absorption occurs by paracellular passive diffusion through tight junctions throughout the entire small intestine — a non-regulated, low-effort route. The transcellular route (Ca²⁺ channels → calbindin → Ca²⁺-ATPase, regulated by calcitriol) is activated when Ca²⁺ intake is LOW.

q: A researcher specifically blocks SGLT1 in an animal model. Which absorption process would be MOST directly impaired?
options: Glucose and galactose uptake from the intestinal lumen
Fructose uptake, since fructose competes with glucose for SGLT1
Dipeptide and tripeptide uptake via PEPT1
Long-chain fatty acid packaging into chylomicrons
Transcellular Ca²⁺ entry via brush-border Ca²⁺ channels
explain: SGLT1 specifically co-transports glucose and galactose with Na⁺. Blocking it directly impairs these two monosaccharides. Fructose uses a separate passive/facilitated transporter independent of SGLT1. PEPT1, chylomicron packaging, and Ca²⁺ channels are entirely distinct systems.

q: Which segment of the small intestine constitutes approximately 5% of its total length?
options: Ileum
Duodenum
Jejunum
Cecum
Sigmoid colon
explain: The duodenum is only ~26 cm long, representing about 5% of the small intestine's total length. The jejunum accounts for ~40% and the ileum the remainder. The cecum and sigmoid colon are parts of the large intestine, not the small intestine.

q: Which anatomical feature of the small intestine provides the LARGEST per-unit surface-area amplification?
options: Circular folds
Microvilli
Villi
Tight junctions
Aquaporins
explain: Microvilli multiply the surface area by ×20, which is the largest single multiplier. Villi contribute ×10 and circular folds ×3. Students commonly reverse microvilli and villi — microvilli are the smallest structures yet give the biggest amplification per unit.

q: Dipeptides and tripeptides are the MAJOR pathway for protein absorption. Which transporter and driving ion are responsible for their uptake at the apical membrane?
options: SGLT1; downhill Na⁺ flux
PEPT1; inward H⁺ flux
A Na⁺-dependent AA transporter; Na⁺ gradient
A basolateral facilitated diffusion carrier; no ion coupling
A Ca²⁺-ATPase; ATP hydrolysis
explain: PEPT1 is an H⁺-coupled peptide transporter: the inward H⁺ gradient (generated by Na⁺/H⁺ exchange at the brush border) drives di/tripeptide uptake. SGLT1 (Na⁺-coupled) handles glucose/galactose; Na⁺-dependent transporters handle free AAs; facilitated diffusion mediates basolateral AA exit.

q: What is the role of mixed micelles in lipid absorption?
options: They directly fuse with absorptive cell membranes and inject lipids
They keep hydrophobic lipid products in aqueous solution and replenish free molecules at the brush border
They hydrolyse triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides
They package long-chain fatty acids into chylomicrons for lymph transport
They activate SGLT1 to co-transport lipids with Na⁺
explain: Mixed micelles (bile salts, phospholipids, fatty acids, monoglycerides) solubilise hydrophobic lipids and are in dynamic equilibrium with free molecules in solution. It is the free molecules — not the micelles themselves — that passively diffuse into absorptive cells. Micelles do not fuse with or enter the cell membrane.

q: A 14-carbon saturated fatty acid is absorbed by the small intestine. Which route does it take after entering the absorptive cell?
options: Directly into the portal vein as a free fatty acid
Packaged into chylomicrons → expelled into central lacteal → lymph
Stored permanently in lipid droplets within the absorptive cell
Converted to glucose via gluconeogenesis in the smooth ER
Transported via aquaporins into the interstitial fluid
explain: A 14-carbon fatty acid is long-chain (>12C). Long-chain fatty acids are re-synthesised into triglycerides in the ER, packaged with apolipoproteins into chylomicrons in the Golgi, and expelled by exocytosis into the central lacteal (lymph). Short-chain (<12C) fatty acids go directly to the portal vein.

q: Along the intestine, where is the rate of sodium (Na⁺) absorption HIGHEST?
options: Duodenum
Jejunum
Ileum
Ascending colon
Descending colon
explain: Na⁺ is absorbed along the entire intestine, but the highest absorption rate occurs in the jejunum. Rates are progressively slower in the ileum and colon. The jejunum is also the primary site of water absorption, which is osmotically driven by Na⁺.

q: When dietary calcium intake is LOW, calcium is absorbed by which route and in which specific location?
options: Paracellular (passive) diffusion throughout the entire small intestine
Transcellular (active) transport in the duodenum and upper jejunum
Transcellular (active) transport in the ileum and large intestine
Paracellular (passive) diffusion in the ascending colon
Aquaporin-mediated transport in the jejunum
explain: When dietary Ca²⁺ is low, the body upregulates transcellular active absorption specifically in the duodenum and upper jejunum via three steps: Ca²⁺ channels at the brush border, calbindin shuttling through the cytosol, and Ca²⁺-ATPase export at the basolateral membrane. High dietary Ca²⁺ uses the simpler paracellular passive route.

q: Fat-soluble nutrients that form large chylomicrons drain into the venous circulation via which route?
options: Portal vein → liver directly
Hepatic vein → inferior vena cava
Central lacteal → lymphatic vessels → venous circulation
Superior mesenteric vein → portal vein
Inferior vena cava directly via tight junctions
explain: Chylomicrons are too large for blood capillaries and enter the central lacteal. The lymphatic system eventually drains into the venous circulation. Water-soluble nutrients (glucose, amino acids, short-chain fatty acids) use the portal vein and go to the liver first.

q: Which brush-border enzyme hydrolyses sucrose into glucose and fructose?
options: Isomaltase
Maltase
Sucrase
Lactase
Amylase
explain: Sucrase specifically cleaves sucrose into glucose and fructose. Isomaltase acts on oligosaccharides with α-1,6 linkages; maltase on maltose/maltotriose; lactase on lactose. Amylase is a luminal enzyme (salivary/pancreatic), not a brush-border enzyme.

q: What is the PRIMARY energy source that drives glucose uptake from the intestinal lumen into absorptive cells via SGLT1?
options: Hydrolysis of ATP directly by SGLT1
An inward K⁺ gradient established by K⁺ channels
The downhill electrochemical gradient of Na⁺ into the cell
The uphill flux of Cl⁻ out of the cell
Phosphorylation of glucose by hexokinase at the brush border
explain: SGLT1 is a secondary active transporter that does not hydrolyse ATP itself. It exploits the Na⁺ electrochemical gradient maintained by the basolateral Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase — Na⁺ flows downhill into the cell, and this energy drives glucose/galactose uphill. K⁺ and Cl⁻ gradients and hexokinase are not coupled to SGLT1.

q: How do free amino acids exit absorptive cells across the basolateral membrane into the blood?
options: Secondary active transport driven by Na⁺ gradient
Primary active transport by an AA-ATPase
Facilitated diffusion (passive carrier-mediated transport)
Simple diffusion directly through the lipid bilayer
Exocytosis packaged in membrane vesicles
explain: Free AAs exit the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion (passive), not active transport. Apical entry of free AAs is Na⁺-dependent (secondary active). This asymmetry is a common exam trap — active on the apical side, passive on the basolateral side.

q: Triglycerides account for approximately what proportion of dietary lipid?
explain: Triglycerides (triacylglycerols) constitute ~90% of dietary lipid. The remaining ~10% includes cholesterol esters, phospholipids, and fat-soluble vitamins. This proportion matters because different lipid classes use similar but not identical absorption pathways.

q: Which enzymes digest triglycerides into free fatty acids and monoglycerides in the GI tract?
options: Salivary amylase and pancreatic amylase
Brush-border peptidases and pancreatic proteases
Gastric lipase and pancreatic lipase
Bile salts and cholesterol esterase
Isomaltase and sucrase
explain: Triglycerides are digested by gastric lipase (stomach) and pancreatic lipase (small intestine lumen) to yield free fatty acids and monoglycerides. Bile salts aid emulsification but are not enzymes. Amylases digest carbohydrates; peptidases digest proteins; bile salts/cholesterol esterase is a classic distractor.

q: In a patient with complete bile duct obstruction (no bile reaching the intestine), which absorption process would be MOST severely compromised?
options: Glucose absorption via SGLT1
Dipeptide absorption via PEPT1
Fat-soluble vitamin absorption (A, D, E, K) via micelle-dependent diffusion
Water absorption by osmosis following Na⁺
Ca²⁺ absorption via the calcitriol-regulated transcellular route
explain: Bile salts are essential for forming mixed micelles, which solubilise hydrophobic lipid digestion products. Without bile, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) and long-chain fatty acids cannot form micelles and cannot be absorbed efficiently. Glucose, dipeptide, water, and transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption do not require bile salts.

q: Water absorption in the small intestine is best described as:
options: Primary active transport driven by ATP hydrolysis at water-ATPases
Secondary active transport coupled directly to Na⁺ entry via SGLT1
Facilitated diffusion through Na⁺-gated channels
Passive osmosis following absorption of osmotically active solutes
Endocytosis of luminal fluid into absorptive cells
explain: Water moves by osmosis, following the absorption of osmotically active solutes (primarily Na⁺, but also glucose and amino acids). It crosses the epithelium through tight junctions (paracellular) and aquaporins on plasma membranes. Water absorption is entirely passive and is maximal in the jejunum, not the duodenum.

q: How many segments does the colon have, and which option lists them correctly?
options: Two: ascending and descending
Three: ascending, transverse, and sigmoid
Three: ascending, transverse, and descending
Four: ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid
Five: ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, and rectal
explain: The colon has exactly four segments: ascending, transverse, descending, and sigmoid. The cecum and rectum are separate components of the large intestine but are NOT segments of the colon. Many students list only three segments — the sigmoid colon is most commonly omitted.

q: Which of the following is NOT a function of the duodenum?
options: Neutralisation of gastric acid by pancreatic juice
Initiation of digestion by pancreatic enzymes and bile
Secretion of H₂O, electrolytes, and mucus from submucosal intestinal glands
Serving as the major site of nutrient absorption (~40% of SI length)
Some nutrient absorption at its lower portion
explain: The jejunum — not the duodenum — accounts for ~40% of small intestinal length and is the MAJOR site of nutrient absorption. The duodenum performs acid neutralisation, initiates digestion, secretes fluids from submucosal glands, and absorbs some nutrients at its lower part, but represents only 5% of SI length.

q: After dipeptides and tripeptides enter absorptive cells, what happens before their constituent amino acids cross the basolateral membrane?
options: They are re-exported intact into lymph via exocytosis
They are phosphorylated and stored in secretory vesicles
They are re-assembled into larger proteins in the Golgi apparatus
They are converted to free amino acids by intracellular peptidases
They are directly secreted as intact peptides into the portal vein
explain: Intracellular peptidases inside absorptive cells cleave di/tripeptides into free amino acids, which then exit the basolateral membrane by facilitated diffusion into the blood. This is why the portal vein carries free amino acids, not intact peptides.

q: Which of the following correctly compares the absorption efficiency of free amino acids versus dipeptides/tripeptides?
options: Free AAs are absorbed faster and are the major protein absorption pathway
Both use PEPT1 (H⁺-coupled) and are absorbed with equal efficiency
Free AAs use H⁺ coupling; dipeptides/tripeptides use Na⁺ coupling
Dipeptides/tripeptides are absorbed more efficiently and are the major pathway; free AAs are slower
Both are absorbed exclusively by endocytosis in adults
explain: Dipeptides and tripeptides are absorbed faster and more efficiently than free amino acids and represent the major pathway for dietary protein absorption. Free AAs are transported more slowly by Na⁺-dependent apical transporters. PEPT1 (H⁺-coupled) is specific to di/tripeptides; free AAs use a distinct Na⁺-coupled transporter.

q: A brush-border enzyme deficiency prevents hydrolysis of oligosaccharides with α-1,6 glycosidic linkages. Which enzyme is deficient?
options: Lactase; galactose and glucose cannot be released from lactose
Sucrase; fructose and glucose cannot be released from sucrose
Maltase; glucose cannot be released from maltose
Isomaltase; glucose cannot be released from branched oligosaccharides
Amylase; starch cannot be cleaved to oligosaccharides in the lumen
explain: Isomaltase specifically cleaves α-1,6 glycosidic linkages in branched oligosaccharides (the branch points from starch digestion), releasing glucose. Without it, these branched fragments accumulate in the lumen. Maltase acts on α-1,4 linkages in maltose/maltotriose; sucrase on sucrose; lactase on lactose. Amylase is a luminal enzyme, not a brush-border enzyme.

q: Which vitamin requires intrinsic factor for intestinal absorption, distinguishing it from all other water-soluble vitamins?
options: Vitamin C
Thiamine (B1)
Riboflavin (B2)
Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine)
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin)
explain: Vitamin B12 is the only water-soluble vitamin that requires intrinsic factor (secreted by gastric parietal cells) for absorption. Without intrinsic factor, B12 cannot be absorbed and pernicious anaemia results. All other B-complex vitamins and Vitamin C use specific membrane carrier proteins without an additional cofactor.

q: A patient with lactose intolerance experiences bloating and diarrhoea after drinking milk. Which pair of mechanisms best explains these symptoms?
options: Lactase excess → rapid glucose absorption → hypertonic blood → osmotic diarrhoea
Lactose activates mucosal mast cells → histamine release → intestinal secretion
Undigested lactose is directly absorbed → triggers hepatic toxicity → GI symptoms
Lactase breaks down lactose into toxic byproducts that irritate the mucosa
Undigested lactose draws water osmotically; colonic bacteria ferment lactose producing lactic acid and gas
explain: Without lactase, lactose stays in the lumen and draws water by osmosis (fluid accumulation, diarrhoea). Colonic bacteria ferment undigested lactose, producing lactic acid, CO₂, H₂, and methane (bloating, cramps, gas, nausea). Lactose intolerance is enzymatic/osmotic/fermentation-based, not immunological or hepatotoxic.

q: Which segments of the large intestine are primarily responsible for storing feces prior to defecation?
options: Cecum and ascending colon
Ascending and transverse colons
Transverse and descending colons
Rectum and cecum
Descending and sigmoid colons
explain: The descending and sigmoid colons are primarily feces storage sites. The ascending and transverse colons perform the bulk of remaining water and electrolyte absorption and secretion. Once feces reaches the rectum (a separate structure, not a colon segment), the defecation reflex is triggered.

q: Calcitriol (active Vitamin D) regulates transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption. Which steps of the transcellular pathway does it regulate?
options: Only the Ca²⁺ channel at the brush border (Step 1)
Only calbindin expression in the cytosol (Step 2)
Only the Ca²⁺-ATPase at the basolateral membrane (Step 3)
Steps 1 and 3 only (channel and pump, not calbindin)
All three steps: Ca²⁺ channel, calbindin, and Ca²⁺-ATPase
explain: Calcitriol upregulates all three steps of transcellular Ca²⁺ absorption: (1) brush-border Ca²⁺ channels, (2) calbindin synthesis in the cytosol, and (3) the basolateral Ca²⁺-ATPase. A common exam trap is assuming it regulates only one or two steps. Note that high dietary Ca²⁺ bypasses all three steps by using the paracellular route.
